Facts.text: Richarius becomes bishop of Liège
Flood of the Arno River, causing massive damage in Florence as recorded by the Florentine chronicler Giovanni Villani
Philip de Blank & Juana "la Loca" depart to Spain
Mamluk Sultan Al-Ashraf Qansuh al-Ghuri sends an expedition of 1100 men to oppose Portuguese expansion in India and safeguard passage to Mecca
Flood ravages Dutch and Friese coast
English cardinal Thomas Wolsey arrested on charges of treason
Eighty Years' War: In Flanders, the Spanish defeat the Walloons and capture Antwerp
Frederick V crowned king of Bohemia
Ferdinand of Austria installed as land guardian of South Netherlands
Massachusetts uses death penalty for denying that Holy Bible is God's word
Storm hits Western Europe: flood in Amsterdam
The Teatro di San Carlo in Naples is inaugurated
Māori Chiefs Hongi Hika and Rewa sell 13,000 acres (5260 hectares) at Kerikeri to the Church Missionary Society for 48 felling axes, New Zealand
The Newport Rising is the last large-scale armed rebellion against authority in mainland Britain
First nationally observed uniform election day in the United States, the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November
American inventor Benjamin Palmer from Meredith, New Hampshire, patents the artificial leg
Count Camillo Benso di Cavour becomes the prime minister of Piedmont-Sardinia, which soon expands to become Italy
Lighthouse built on Alcatraz Island, San Francisco Bay
University of Washington founded in Seattle
Battle at Reynoldsburg Island near Johnsonville, Tennessee: Confederate troops bombard Union supplies and fleet destroying four gunboats
Confederate assault on Johnsonville, Tennessee
Kingdom of Italy annexes Venetia
90 kegs of powder used to get rock from Telegraph Hill, San Francisco, for seawall
Dentist John Beers of San Francisco patents the gold crown
Samuel J. Tilden elected governor of New York
Massachusetts Rifle Association is founded in Boston
Passenger Steamship "Pacific" collides with sailing vessel "Orpheus" off Cape Flattery, Washington, 236 die
Tonga adopts constitution
James Ritty patents first cash register, to combat stealing by bartenders in his saloon in Dayton, Ohio
Edward MacDowell's orchestra suite "Ophelia" premieres
Players League begins, declaring independence from baseball's NL
Alexander Borodin's opera "Prince Igor" debuts at Mariinsky Theatre, St. Petersburg, Russia
Great Britain proclaims Zanzibar as a protectorate
Prince of Wales opens first underground station at Stockwell, South London
British South Africa Company troops under Dr Jamesons occupy Bulawayo, Matabeleland
Panama and Colombia wake up to news that the insurrectionists have declared an independent Republic of Panama
First stadium built specifically for football (Harvard Stadium)
Dock strike against importing grain elevators in Rotterdam
Brooklyn Academy of Music opens in NYC
Start of South Africa's 1st F-C game in Aust (v S Aust). It rained
France & Germany sign treaty about Morocco & Congo
Vogue holds 1st model show ("Fashion Fete", NYC)
The Allied armistice with Austria-Hungary, signed 3 November, goes into effect
Japanese Prime Minister Hara Takashi is assassinated by a right wing fanatic in Tokyo
California legalizes professional boxing (illegal since 1914)
Miriam (Ma) Ferguson one of the first two women elected US governor (of Texas) [See also Nellie Tayloe Ross, of Wyoming]
Nellie Tayloe Ross one of the first two women elected US governor (Wyoming) [See also Miriam (Ma) Ferguson, of Texas]
Jose Moncada elected president of Nicaragua
Titanic Thompsonparticipates in a high-stakes poker game that leads to the murder of Arnold Rothstein
John L. Balderston's play "Berkeley Square" starring Leslie Howard premieres in NYC
Legendary New Zealand-Australian racehorse Phar Lap wins the Melbourne Cup, with the shortest odds ever offered
Young Park (1) in the Bronx named in honor of James Young

After posting 7 straight shutouts to start the NFL season, Detroit Lions beat Pittsburgh, 40-7 at University of Detroit Stadium; scored upon for the first time of the season; rush for NFL single game record 426 yards
First air conditioned automobile (Packard) exhibited, Chicago, Illinois
US allows "cash & carry" arms sales during WW II
Eggs & cake rationed in Netherlands
13th day of battle at El Alamein: Afrika Korps draws back
German troops reconquer Schmidt Hurtgenwald
RAF bombs Dinteloord (Netherlands), 54 killed
United Nations Educational, Scientific, & Cultural Organization (UNESCO) formed
"One Man's Family" premieres on TV in the US, after running as a radio show since 1932
Indonesian troops reconquer Ambon
US troops vacate Pyongyang, North Korea
NY Giants & NY Yanks score back-to-back TDs on kickoff returns
Vijay Merchant scores 154 v England in his last Test Cricket innings
Earthquake & flood strike Kamshatka-South America
New baseball balk rule gives the batter option of accepting the outcome of the pitch or the balk
Philadelphia A's move to Kansas City
200,000 Russian troops attack anti-Soviet movement in Budapest, Hungary
2nd Soviet Earth-satellite launched
Democrats win US congressional election
Konstantinos Karamanlis becomes premier of Greece
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Johnston Island
American pianist and composer Terry Riley and musicians premiere his work "In C" at Tape Music Center, San Francisco; the piece is often cited as the first minimalist masterpiece
Lee Breedlove sets female land speed record (308.56 MPH) in Utah
Flooding of Arno River (Florence, Italy) destroys countless art works, kills 113
Battles between Jordanian army & Al Fatah-arm forces
WRDU (now WPTF) TV channel 28 in Raleigh-Durham, NC (NBC) 1st broadcast
WTOG TV channel 44 in St Petersburg-Tampa, FL (IND) 1st broadcast
Genie, a 13-year-old feral child is found in Los Angeles, California, having been locked in her bedroom by her father for most of her life
"I Can See Clearly Now" single by Johnny Nash begins a 3-week run on the top of the charts
Bangladesh adopts constitution
Kings score 3 goals within 45 seconds against Islanders
Mark Medoff's "When You Comin' Back, Red Ryder?" premieres in NYC
New Orleans Saints 1st shutout victory, 13-0 vs Buffalo Bills
The Netherlands experiences the first Car Free Sunday caused by the 1973 oil crisis. Highways are deserted and are solely used by cyclists and roller skaters.
Thousands commemorate former Greek premier Georgios Papandreou
UN Security council proclaims weapon embargo against South Africa
Iranian troops fire on anti-Shah student protesters by Tehran University
Islanders start 15 game undefeated streak (12-0-3)
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Islanders start 15 game undefeated streak (13-0-2)
Libyan invasion in Chad
Sadaharu Oh, 40, professional baseball's all-time home run king with 868, retires
Beth Henley's "Crimes of the Heart" premieres in NYC
Columbia shuttle launch scrubbed with 31 secs remaining
Dr George Nichopoulas is acquitted of overprescribing addictive drugs for Elvis Presley
Ruud Lubbers becomes Dutch premier
Bomb attack on Israeli headquarter in Tyrus Lebanon, 60 killed
Washington Capitals first NHL overtime victory beating Vancouver 5-4
Nicaragua holds first free elections in 56 years; Sandinistas win 63%
"Edge of Darkness" first airs on BBC Two, featuring Bob Peck and Joanne Whalley
Benito Santiago, Padres catcher, wins NL Rookie of Year
Lisa Steinberg, battered into coma by her adoptive father Joel
NBA announces four new franchises: Charlotte and Miami for 1988, and Minneapolis and Orlando for 1989
First NBA game at Charlotte Coliseum - Hornets lose to Cavs, 133-93
First NBA game at Orlando Arena, Orlando Magic loses to NJ Nets, 111-106
Rene Muawad elected president of Lebanon
The congress of the Solidarity Party is inaugurated in Sweden. The congress decides, contrary to the proposal of the central committee, not to disband the party.
Iraq says it is preparing for a "dangerous war"
US Secretary of State James Baker visits American troops in Saudi Arabia
Mid East peace conference ends in Madrid Spain
NY Giants announce they will quit WNEW Radio after 32 years for WOR
NY Jets announce they are moving from WABC to WFAN radio
Bolivia becomes a member of the Berne Convention copyright treaty
San Francisco: First conference that focuses exclusively on the subject of the commercial potential of the World Wide Web.
Soyuz TM-20 lands in Kazakhstan
United Center in Chicago opens - Bulls beat Charlotte Hornets, 89-83
KeyArena opens in Seattle, Supersonics beat the LA Lakers 103-89
Chinese authorities arrest cyber-dissident He Depu for signing pro-democracy letter to the 16th Communist Party Congress
Former HealthSouth CEO Richard Scrushy becomes the first person indicted under the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. He was eventually acquitted.
The most powerful solar flare as observed by satellite instrumentation is recorded.
12 French soldiers, 3 UN personnel and hundreds of civilians die during the Côte d'Ivoire civil war.
American "Deftones" bassist Chi Cheng (38) suffers serious injuries in an automobile crash in Santa Clara, California, as a seatbelt-less passenger, dying 4-1/2 years later
Bishop Tawadros appointed as the 118th Pope of the Coptic Orthodox Church
Syrian rebels capture a major oil field in Deir ez-Zor Governorate
Protectionist wins the 2014 Melbourne Cup
The body of the last missing victim is found on board the wrecked Costa Concordia, during its dismantling in Genoa
The US votes in mid-term elections: Republicans retain the house & regain the Senate
Tim Scott becomes the first African-American Senator in the south since the Reconstruction
Plane crashes after take-off from Juba international airport, South Sudan, killing 37, 1-year-old survives in father's arms
Romanian Prime Minister Victor Ponta announces his resignation after protests over a Bucharest nightclub fire that killed 32
Paris Agreement on climate change becomes effective
Lebanese Prime Minister Saad Hariri resigns in a shock TV broadcast from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, amid concerns he is being forcibly detained
Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art in Nusantara opens in Jakarta with items from the collection of Indonesian tycoon Haryanto Adikoesoemo
Saudi Arabia arrests 11 princes and other ministers on corruption charges, including billionaire investor Prince Alwaleed bin Talal
Scottish champions Celtic sets new all-time British record (63) for matches undefeated in all domestic football competitions beating St. Johnstone, 4-0 at McDiarmid Park
Death toll from week-long storm in Italy rises to 29 with death of nine people in floods in Casteldaccia, Sicily
New York City Marathon: Lelisa Desisa of Ethiopia runs 2:05:59, 2nd fastest men's time in history; Kenya's Mary Keitany captures her 4th women's NYC crown in 2:22:48
Largest mass commutation in US history when 462 non-violent inmates freed from Oklahoma prisons as part of state prison reforms
Major investigation finds unsafe lead levels in 11 Canadian cities between 2014 and 2018 mostly due to antiquated pipes
Nine members of a US Mexican Mormon family, including six children, shot and killed in attack by criminal gang in Northern Mexico
Albino activist Overstone Kondowe is sworn in as Malawi's first MP with albinism
COP26 climate pledges, if kept, could help limit global warning to 1.8 °C (above pre-industrial averages) according to the International Energy Agency
WHO says Europe is again the Epicenter for COVID-19 after cases rise 50% in a month
Astronomers announce discovery of closest known black hole to Earth, just 1,600 light-years away, 10x more massive that our sun
A new survey of Japanese teenagers reveals only a fifth of  teenage boys aged 15-18-years-old have experienced their first kiss, the lowest level since 1974
Roman Senator Priscus Attalus appointed Emperor of the West by Gothic leaderAlaric I, in opposition to Emperor Honorius, while Rome is under siege by the Goths
Eleven-year-oldCharles VIis crowned King of France at Reims Cathedral
Joan of Arcand Charles d'Albret liberate the heavily fortified town of Saint-Pierre-le-Moûtier after a siege
Danish Norwegian KingChristian IIcrowned King of Sweden
PrinceFrederick Henryconquers Hulst, the last major siege of the Eighty Years' War
Carlo Goldoni's prose comedy "Le Bourru Bienfaisant" premieres in Paris
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart's "Symphony No. 36" premieres in Linz, Austria
James Buchananelected 15th US president
American inventorRichard Jordan Gatlingpatents the hand-cranked Gatling machine gun in Indianapolis
Johannes Brahms' 1st Symphony in C premieres in Karlsruhe, Grand Duchy of Baden
African American inventorThomas Elkinspatents the Refrigerating Apparatus
Grover Cleveland(D) beatsJames G. Blaine(R) for his 1st presidential term (only American president to serve 2 non-consecutive terms)
Ermanno Wolf-Ferrari's opera "Il Segreto di Susanna" (Susanna's Secret) is first produced in Munich, Germany
Russian TsarNicholas IIvisits German EmperorWilhelm IIat Potsdam; they force tentative agreements on spheres of influence in the Middle East
The Sturmabteilung or SA (the "Brown Shirts") is formally established byAdolf Hitler
Calvin Coolidgeis elected to a full term as President of the United States, defeating Democrat candidate John W. Davis
Stanley Baldwinbecomes Britain's Prime Minister for a second time after a landslide victory overRamsay MacDonald's Labour Party
American gangsterArnold Rothsteinis shot at a business meeting for reputedly refusing to pay gambling debts (dies 6th November)

Richard E. Byrd, Laurence McKinley Gould and their polar expedition team begin a 2½ month, 1500-mile dog-sledge journey into the Queen Maud Mountains. The first exploration of the interior of Antarctica.
Jean Genet's play "Judith" premieres in Paris
Bulgarian communist Georgi Dimitrov defends himself and the ideology of communism againstHermann Goeringand his Nazi accusers on charges of setting the Reichstag on fire
American humoristWill Rogerscommemorated by US Postage Service on 3-cent stamp
American-born British poetT. S. Eliotwins Nobel Prize for literature
Republican candidateDwight D. Eisenhoweris elected President of the United States, defeating Democrat candidateAdlai Stevenson
Angelo Giuseppe Roncalli crowned as PopeJohn XXIII
Belgian minority government ofGaston Eyskensresigns
Ernie Banks, Cubs shortstop, wins 2nd consecutive NL MVP
Mary LeakeyandLouis Leakeydiscover first Homo habilis jaw fragments (OH 7) at Olduvai Gorge, Tanzania
American folk singer-songwriterBob Dylanmakes his Carnegie Hall (NYC) debut; tickets priced at $2.00

John Lennonutters his famous quip at a Royal Variety Performance "For our last number, I'd like to ask your help.  Will the people in the cheaper seats clap your hands? And for the rest of you, if you’ll just rattle your jewelry…" at the Prince of Wales Theatre in London
British rock bandPink Floydmake US live debut, sharing bill with Big Brother & The Holding Company, and singer-songwriter Richie Havens, at the Winterland Auditorium, San Francisco, California
Capitol Records releases "Wichita Lineman", the 12th album byGlen Campbell(Billboard Album of the Year, 1969)

Northern Ireland Prime MinisterTerence O'Neillmeets British Prime MinisterHarold Wilsonfor talks on Northern Ireland; Wilson states no change of constitutional position of Northern Ireland possible without consent of the its people
Russian nuclear physicistAndrei Sakharovforms Human Rights Committee
Chicago centreStan Mikitaregisters his 1,000 NHL career point in the Black Hawks 5-3 loss to the Minnesota North Stars at the Met Centre, Bloomington, Minnesota
OriolesJim Palmerwins his 2nd Cy Young Award
1st mass-market free-agent reentry draft,Reggie Jackson, Joe Rudi, Gullett, Tenace, Fingers, Baylor, Grich & McCovey, available
3rd annual reentry free-agent draft,Pete Rose,Tommy John, & Darrell Evans, available
MLB Philadelphia PhilliesSteve Carltonwins 3rd NL Cy Young Award
Republican candidateRonald Reaganis elected President of the United States, defeating incumbent Democrat US PresidentJimmy Carterby a landslide
Warner Bros. Records releases "Hearts and Bones", the sixth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon
Funk-rocker Prince opens his "Purple Rain" tour featuring his new band 'The Revolution' with the 1st of seven shows at the Joe Louis Arena in Detroit, Michigan
Ayrton Sennaretires with gearbox trouble during the season-ending Australian Grand Prix in Adelaide; wins by 7 points fromAlain Prost; McLaren-Honda wins Constructors title for 3rd straight year
Imelda Marcosreturns from exile to the Philippines and was arrested the next day for tax fraud and corruption and soon released on $6,400 bail
Elton Johnawarded $518,700 from Sunday Mirror for a false report on his diet
Jean Chretienis appointed the Prime Minister of Canada by Governor General Ray Hnatyshyn
British girl group theSpice Girlsrelease their debut album "Spice", goes on to sell 23 million copies
Mercury Nashville Records releases "Come On Over", the 3rd studio album by country music singerShania Twain(Billboard Album of the Year, 1999)

"Harry Potterand the Philosopher's Stone," the first film adaptation of the book series byJ. K. Rowling, starringDaniel Radcliffe, premieres in London (titled "Harry Potterand the Sorcerer's Stone" in some markets)
53rd Emmy Awards:The West Wing, Sex and the City,James Gandolfini&Edie Falcowin
American tennis starSerena Williamswins the WTA Tour Championship in Munich, Germany afterLindsay Davenportdefaults the final due to a knee injury; Williams' first season-ending title
"Shock'n Y'all" 8th studio album byToby Keithis released (Billboard Album of the Year 2004)
Barack Obamabecomes the first African-American to be elected President of the United States, defeating Republican candidateJohn McCain
Ukraine PresidentPetro Poroshenkoorders army reinforcements to key southern and eastern cities to combat potential rebel offensive
Justin Trudeausworn in as Canadian Prime Minister with a cabinet split equally between men and women
Katy Perryis the highest paid musician of the year, earning $135 million, according to Forbes Magazine
British TV series "The Crown" premieres withClaire FoyplayingQueen Elizabeth IIandMatt SmithasPrince Philip
Camila Cabellowins four awards at the MTV Europe Music Awards in Bilbao,Janet Jacksonaccepts the Global Icon award
Ethiopian PMAbiy Ahmedorders military offensive and state of emergency in northern region of Tigray, amid fears of a civil war
Columbia Records releases album "Barbra Streisand- Live At The Bon Soir", recorded in an NYC nightclub in November 1962
First wagon train arrives in California after a five-and-a-half-month, 1,730-mile journey over the Sierra Nevada from Missouri
Howard Carter rediscovers the intact tomb of the PharaohTutankhamunin Egypt
500 Iranian students loyal toAyatollah Khomeiniseize the US Embassy in Tehran, taking 90 hostages for 444 days
Alexanderplatz demonstrations for political reforms draw over half a million people in East Berlin, significant event that led to the fall of the Berlin Wall
Taejo of Joseon [Yi Seong-gye], King of Joseon (1392-98), born in Hamhung, North Korea (d. 1408)
Alphonso II, King of Naples (1494-95), born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (now Italy) (d. 1495)
Guido Reni, Italian Baroque painter, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1642)
Gerard van Honthorst, Dutch painter, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1656)
Mary, 1st British Princess Royal and the Princess of Orange, born in St James's Palace, London (d. 1660)
Carlo Mannelli, Italian composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1697)
Leonard Sailer, German composer and organist, born in the Free Imperial City of Ulm, Holy Roman Empire (d. after 1694)
Charles III Philip, Elector Palatine (1716-42), born in Neuburg an der Donau, Germany (d. 1742)
Anton Englert, German composer and organist, born in the Free Imperial City of  Schweinfurt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1751)
Robert Praelisauer, German composer, music director, and priest, baptized in Kötzting, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1771)

Thomas Johnson, American lawyer, militia commander, politician (delegate to the First Continental Congress, 1st Governor of Maryland, 1977-79), and jurist (US Supreme Court Justice, 1791-93), born in St. Leonard, Maryland, British America (d. 1819)
Augustus Montague Toplady, English hymn writer (Rock of Ages), born in Farnham, Surrey, England (d. 1778)
Anthony van Hoboken, Dutch merchant and ship owner, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1850)
Pierre-Simon Girard, French mathematician and engineer (fluid mechanics), born in Caen, France (d. 1836)
Jan Willem Pieneman, Dutch historical painter (Battle at Waterloo), born in Abcoude, Dutch Republic (d. 1853)
Edmund Kean, British Shakespearean actor (Edmund Kean's Masonic Career), born in London, England (d. 1833)
Carlos Antonio López, 1st President of Paraguay (1844-62), born in Asunción, Paraguay (d. 1862)
Aleksander Bestuzhev-Marlinsky, Russian author, born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1837)
Eduard Brendler, Swedish composer, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1831)
Benjamin Robbins Curtis, American attorney and Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, born in Watertown, Massachusetts (d. 1874)
Aleardo [Gaetano] Aleardi, Italian poet (Lettere a Maria), born in Verona, Austrian Empire (d. 1878)
Stephen Johnson Field, American jurist, Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, born in Haddam, Connecticut (d. 1899)
William Polk Hardeman, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Williamson County, Tennessee (d. 1898)
Alexander Lawton, Brigadier General in the Confederate Army, born in Beaufort District, South Carolina (d. 1896)
Robert Vinkler Richardson, American Brigadier-General (Confederate Army), born in Granville County, North Carolina (d. 1870)
Karel Komzák I, Bohemian organist, conductor, military bandleader, and composer, born in Netěchovice, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austrian Empire  (d. 1893)[1]
Lunsford L. Lomax, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Newport, Rhode Island (d. 1913)
Henry J. Lutcher, American businessman and sawmill partner, born in Williamsport, Pennsylvania (d. 1912)
Carl Tausig, Polish pianist, student and protégé of Liszt, and composer noted for his transcriptions, born in Warsaw, Congress Poland (d. 1871)
Vasudeo Balwant Phadke, Indian independence activist and revolutionary, born in Shirdhon village, Panvel taluka, Raigad district, British India (d. 1883)
Gaston Serpette, French composer, born in Nantes, France (d. 1904)
Stanisław Niewiadomski, Polish composer, born in Soposzyn, Kingdom of Galicia, Austrian Empire (d. 1936)
Eden Phillpotts, English novelist, poet and playwright (Red Madymaynes), born in Mount Abu, Rajasthan, India (d. 1960)
William Faulkes, British organist, transcriber and composer, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1933)
Tomasz Arciszewski, 31st Prime Minister of Poland, 3rd Prime Minister of the Polish government-in-exile in London (1944-47), born in Sierzchowy, Piotrków Governorate, Congress Poland (d. 1955)
Carolina “La Belle” Otero, Spanish actress, singer and courtesan, born in Valga, Galicia, Spain (d. 1965)
Herman Finck, British conductor (Palace Theatre (London), 1900-20), theater and light classical composer (In The Shadows), born in London (d. 1939)
Bobby Wallace, Baseball HOF shortstop, pitcher (Temple Cup 1895 Cleveland Spiders) and manager (St. Louis Browns, Cincinnati Reds), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1960)
Aleksandr Vasilevich Kolchak, Russian military commander, born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1920)
James Earle Fraser, American sculptor (Indian Head (Buffalo) nickel), born in Winona, Minnesota (d. 1953)
Gena Branscombe, Canadian pianist and composer, born in Picton, Ontario (d. 1977)
Milton Rosmer, British director (Murder in the Red Barn), born in Southport, Lancashire, England (d. 1971)
Knut Algot Håkanson, Swedish composer, born in Kinna, Sweden (d. 1929)
Alton Adams, American composer (1st black bandmaster in the United States Navy), born in St. Thomas, Virgin Islands (d. 1987)
Klabund [Alfred Henschke], German writer (Bracke, Borgia), born in Crossen, Germany (d. 1928)
Miroslav Krejčí, Czech composer, born in Rychnov nad Kněžnou, Austria-Hungary (d. 1964)
Carlos P. Garcia, 8th President of the Philippines (1957-61), born in Talibon, Bohol, Philippines (d. 1971)
Ian Wolfe, American actor (Diary of a Madman, Wizards & Warriors, Houdini, THX-1138, Homebodies), born in Canton, Illinois (d. 1992)
Oscar Lorenzo Fernández, Brazilian conductor and composer (Imbapara), born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1948)
Mimi Boesnach, Dutch actress (Wedding of Kloris & Roosje), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Nicolas Frantz, Luxembourgish road cyclist (Tour de France 1927-28), born in Mamer, Capellen, Luxembourg (d. 1985)
Don Alvarado [Jose Page], American actor (Captain Thunder, Morning Glory, Big Steal), born in Albuquerque, New Mexico (d. 1967)
Lucreţiu Pătrăşcanu, Romanian communist activist and sociologist, born in Bacău, Romania (d. 1954)
Luigi Lucioni, Italian-American landscape painter (opera stars), born in Malnate, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1988)
Spyridon Marinatos, Greek archaeologist who rediscovered the site of an ancient port city on the island of Thera, in the southern Aegean Sea, born in Lixoúrion, Cephalonia, Greece (d. 1974)
Yi Bangja [Princess Masako of Nashimoto], Japanese royal (Crown Princess Euimin of Korea, 1926-70), known as the "mother of the handicapped in Korea", born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1989)
Frank Jenks, American stage and screen actor (Colonel Flack, Zombies on Broadway), born in Des Moines, Iowa (d. 1962)
Ion Vasilescu, Romanian composer, born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1960)
Tadeusz Żyliński, Polish technician and textilist, born in Vilnius, Lithuania (d. 1967)
Walter Bauer, German Canadian writer, born in Merseburg (d. 1976)
Martin Raschke, German author and publisher, born in Dresden, Germany (d. 1943)
Arnold Cooke, British composer, born in Gomersal, West Yorkshire, England (d. 2005)
Bob Considine, American journalist and author (Babe Ruth Story), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1975)
Roswell Leavitt Gilpatric, American lawyer and diplomat, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1996)
Siegfried Borris, German composer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1987)
Anthony Warde, American actor (Dangers of the Canadian Mounted, Buck Rogers, Black Widow), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1975)
Joseph Rotblat, Polish Physicist, (Nobel Peace Prize 1995 for work against nuclear arms), born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 2005)
Pauline Trigère, French-American fashion designer (Bell Bottoms), born in Paris (d. 2002)
Stanley Cortez, American cinematographer (Magnificent Ambersons), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Bert Patenaude, American soccer forward (4 caps; scorer of first hat-trick in World Cup history, 1930), born in Fall River, Massachusetts (d. 1974)
Evelyn Johnson, American pilot (most flying hours - 57,685), born in Corbin, Kentucky (d. 2012)
Jack Rose, American screenwriter, born in Warsaw, Russian Empire (d. 1995)
Osamu Shimizu, Japanese composer, born in Osaka, Japan (d. 1986)
Giff Vivian, New Zealand cricket all-rounder (7 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 100, 17 wickets; Auckland), born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 1983)
Vadim Salmanov, Russian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1978)
Gig Young [Byron Elsworth Barr], American actor (They Shoot Horses Don't They), born in St. Cloud, Minnesota (d. 1978)
Carlos Castillo Armas, Dictator and President of Guatemala (1954-57), born in Santa Lucía Cotzumalguapa, Guatemala (d. 1957)
Alistair Cameron Crombie, Australian historian of science, born in Brisbane, Australia (d. 1996)
Court Benson, Canadian-American actor and broadcaster, born in Vancouver, British Columbia (d. 1995)
Lloyd James Austin, Australian linguist and literary scholar, born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1994)
John Basilone, US Marine (Medal of Honor at the Battle for Henderson Field), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1945)
Ruth Handler [née Mosko], American toy manufacturer (invented the Barbie doll), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 2002)
Leonardo Cimino, Italian-American actor (The Monster Squad, V, Dune, Waterworld, Hudson Hawk), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2012)
Cameron Mitchell, American actor (The High Chaparral), born in Dallastown, Pennsylvania (d. 1994)
Martin Balsam, American character actor (Psycho, 12 Angry Men, Archie Bunker's Place, Catch 22), born in New York City (d. 1996)
Georges Papy, Belgian mathematician (Numbers Game), born in Anderlecht, Belgium (d. 2011)
Antonio Ruiz Soler, Spanish flamenco dancer and choreographer, born in Seville, Spain (d. 1996)
Benno Besson, Swiss actor and director (Der Arzt wider Willen), born in Yverdon-les-Bains, Vaud, Switzerland (d. 2006)
Fernand Bonnier de La Chapelle, French resistance fighter and assassin of Admiral of the Fleet and Vichy Prime Minister François Darlan, born in Algiers, French Empire (d. 1942)
Poul Rovsing Olsen, Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1982)
Ralph Sutton, American stride and Dixieland jazz pianist and composer, born in Hamburg, Missouri (d. 2001)
Freddy Heineken, Dutch beer brewer (Heineken), born in Amsterdam (d. 2002)

Howie Meeker, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame right wing, coach, broadcaster (NHL All Star 1947-49; Stanley Cup 1947, 48, 49, 51; Toronto Maple Leafs) and politician (Member of Parliament), born in Kitchener, Ontario (d. 2020)
Kjerstin Dellert, Swedish opera singer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 2018)
Ritwik Ghatak, Bengali filmmaker (Titash Ekti Nadir Naam), born in Dhaka, East Bengal (d. 1976)
Vittorio Fellegara, Italian composer, born in Milan, Italy (d. 2011)
Hannah Weiner, American experimental poet, born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1997)
Shaike Ophir, Israeli actor, comedian and Israel's 1st mime (The Policeman, The Delta Force), born in Jerusalem, Mandate Palestine (d. 1987)

Dick Groat, American baseball shortstop (NL MVP & NL batting champion 1960 Pittsburgh Pirates; 8 x MLB All-Star; St. Louis Cardinals) and College Basketball HOF guard (Duke Uni; Fort Wayne Pistons), born in Wilkinsburg, Pennsylvania (d. 2023)
Kate Reid, British actress (The Andromeda Strain, Heaven Help US), born in London, England (d. 1993)
